claim: Having clean, structured data upfront is a core competitive advantage for agents; generic agents with tool access are outperformed by domain-specific agents with relevant data context
actionable: Invest in data quality and curation before building agents; contextualize agents with domain-specific data rather than relying solely on external tool integrations (e.g., MCP)
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Cross-checking answers between multiple model families (Claude, GPT, Gemini) improves performance on data-entry and scientific tasks; when models agree, quality is high; disagreement signals errors
actionable: For critical tasks, route the same query to multiple model providers; cross-compare results; treat disagreement as a signal to review with a human
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: In science, most tasks are not easily verifiable; evaluation via production traces and user feedback is more practical than static benchmarks for domain-specific scientific questions
actionable: For scientific agents, focus on production trace analysis rather than building comprehensive eval sets; collect user feedback on agent outputs; build evals only for verifiable sub-tasks (data retrieval, data entry)
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: SQL-based context is effective for agents in data-driven domains; embed table names and descriptions to help models query correctly; balance modern file-based patterns with legacy SQL infrastructure
actionable: If your domain has rich SQL data structures, teach the agent SQL; provide table schema documentation in context; avoid forcing file-system patterns if SQL is native to your domain
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Coding-harness patterns don't transfer directly to science; spec-based testing and defined end-conditions fail because ground truth requires running physical experiments (weeks/months, expensive)
actionable: When applying agent patterns from coding to other domains, test whether your tasks are 'verifiable in silicon'; if not (science, biology), build custom harnesses without spec-based assumptions
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Production traces are essential for tuning agents; establish a weekly fire-chief rotation and team-wide trace review; product managers and engineers should inspect production behavior after releases
actionable: Assign weekly rotation for trace review; make it a standing agenda item; have feature owners inspect traces after deploying changes; gather user thumbs-up/down feedback
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Agents for scientific tasks are intelligence-limited, not token-limited; invest in spending more tokens intelligently (multi-model, multi-agent) because better answers are worth the cost
actionable: In high-stakes domains, prefer token-heavy strategies (multiple model calls, cross-checks) over cost optimization; measure whether more intelligent answers justify increased inference cost
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Build agents with skills as a user-facing primitive that agents can create and update; agents should be able to synthesize repeated patterns into shareable, updatable skills
actionable: Expose skills as a first-class abstraction in your agent interface; allow agents to propose and create new skills from observed patterns; provide examples and documentation
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Background agents running on schedule (weekly reports) or triggered by events (instrument data arrival) accelerate repetitive documentation work; pair with human-approval gates
actionable: Identify data-intensive or documentation-heavy processes; trigger agents on data arrival or schedule them for standing meetings; build approval workflows
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Understanding LLMs requires a mindset closer to biology than software engineering; LLMs are black boxes you probe and observe, like biological systems, not deterministic code
actionable: Approach LLM testing like scientific research: form hypotheses, probe behavior, measure responses; avoid assuming deterministic behavior; embrace iterative experimentation
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Models lack understanding of physical-world constraints; tasks requiring translation between hand-done experiments and lab automation are poor fits for agents today
actionable: Identify tasks requiring physical understanding or hardware-domain knowledge; prioritize agent work on data manipulation, analysis, and information retrieval; defer embodied/hardware tasks
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Agents can speed up drug discovery by 2x by eliminating workflow friction (fast data retrieval, analysis) and improving experimental design (DOE); agents are not close to discovering novel drugs independently
actionable: Target agents at workflow acceleration and design guidance; set realistic expectations about what agents can do; measure impact on cycle time, not scientific novelty
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Don't expose underlying models to domain-expert users; instead, invest in figuring out which model is best for each task and route transparently; let users focus on their domain
actionable: Hide model selection complexity from end users; run internal evals to match task difficulty to model capability; route tasks intelligently based on complexity, not user preference
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Certain model families have different strengths (e.g., Gemini for images); when building for specialized tasks, use model-specific strengths while defaulting to multi-model for coverage
actionable: Profile model capabilities on your domain tasks; build specialized routes for image/vision tasks to Gemini; use multi-model for general tasks to increase robustness
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: LLMs are next-token predictors but can innovate with the right prompting and task framing; lack of novelty is often due to insufficient experimentation, not fundamental limits
actionable: Experiment with novelty-focused prompts, tournament-style ideation (multiple agents proposing ideas, evaluating), and hypothesis-driven probing to unlock creative behaviors
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Product-minded engineers with strong listening skills and deep domain understanding are most effective at building agents; science background is not required
actionable: When hiring agent engineers, prioritize product mindset and domain understanding over domain-specific credentials; invest in learning from domain experts through user research
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Fine-tuning in highly specialized domains is difficult; frontier models are improving faster than the benefit of fine-tuning can offset; security and privacy constraints often prevent cross-customer training
actionable: In regulated domains, avoid cross-customer fine-tuning due to privacy risks; for specialized science, test frontier models thoroughly before investing in fine-tuning; measure ROI carefully
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough

claim: Scientists prefer focusing on hard scientific problems, not administrative tasks; agents handling data entry and document generation reduce friction, not intellectual engagement
actionable: Target agents at high-friction, low-creativity tasks (data entry, report compilation, file management); preserve user agency for strategic decisions (experiment design, hypothesis formulation)
source_title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough
